transcript.transcript: History interview conducted with Mrs. Louisa Keyser on 20th April 1979 at Priester, Idaho. Interviewer is Michael R. Anderson. We had to get along, no other way about it, so that was it. Candle lights and... Well candle lights, that was my mother-in-law and father-in-law's when they first started, you know, when they first moved up there on the place that we have still. Oh, yeah, that was in the 1880s. We got the... 1888. Grandpa's gotten somewhere about... Yeah. So that's it, you know, they had to just make the best of it and get along the best way it was to get along. Did you want to hear all your cooking was on a wood stove? Absolutely. A lot of wood. You get wood on hand all winter months. And then when it's summertime, oh dear, harvesting time, you know, had to have a good nice wood fire. Well, no other way. That was it. Bake, I remember I used to get up early in the morning to get my, you know, the bread baking, you know, of course, you know, for the bread and so get it out, you know, before it got too hot, you know, to keep the fire going. Oh yeah, it wasn't nothing to it because that's all we had the way of doing it, it had to be done. That's it. Nowadays, I told you, have somebody else, the push buttons are easier, why not? It's all right. That's good. How many loaves of bread did you bake? Well, I'd say about maybe oh six or seven or eight at the most, about, you know, once a week. Yeah, by the flour, you know, and the following year it gets a couple barrels, what was it, a couple barrels? Four fifty-nine pound, they call it a barrel and a half, a couple or three barrels, you know, for the winter storage, you know, because them days wasn't no taking off with cars, you know, to go downtown any time a person feel like it. Horses and sled, they were, you know. So that's okay. It was fine. It was good because that's the only way that, you know, that's for everybody, Not only, not only one family at them days, because it was for everybody. Yeah. Well, you know, his grandparents, they were, they were living in the middle of Spokane, it's now. You know, Grandpa and Grandma Kaiser before they moved on the, on the whole place. They owned two lots there. Just think, they sold it for, what did I tell you, Grandma sold it, $60. $60 for the two lots, and even a cow included in with it. I remember Grandma and Grandpa, you know, talking about it. No kidding. And then they moved out this way. So, that was it. God knows how many years ago. A hundred years ago. Well, 1888, so it isn't too far from a hundred years. Well, that's right. Yeah. So you see... When he come out here he hit Spokane in 1882. Yeah. Spokane Falls. Well, yeah. Spokane Falls. And then he stayed there till 88 and he had... What I know as far as what I know right to this day that he owned, where the, is it the Paulson building? Where the Paulson building sits today? Go sit down Ma. Go sit down Ma. Okay, but he's gonna tip over, he's got wine for too much. This is an antique piano stool too. He doesn't get off of it. Well he didn't know he needed, he wanted some help. Well, he's got a good boy. Now that's a good boy. Now you can sit down here and listen. Listen to Mike. You told me Mike Anderson was here. Well you know him, don't you? Is that alright? Listen to him. Listen to what he tells us. How many acres did you have down there? On the place? Not including, well of course 165 that was sold here a few years ago to the Merritt brothers. The home plate is 260 some. I think it was 467 altogether. Well, I see the one up there, I think 427, I think, not 627, whatever. But anyhow, anyhow, yeah. Yeah, we sold some, you know, here three, four years ago. Yeah, And it was all done, the hard work, you know, at the early days, of course. You know who this little guy belonged to? That's Phillip's boy. I know who he is. He's the one that does it. He's Papa's boy though, pretty much. He's such a good boy. But I'm still Dad's son. Yeah, I know. He's a dad's son, but you're still a papa's boy. Yeah. You know. Oh, he tells Grandma lots of, lots of, lots of long, That's a good boy. What did you raise down there instead? Well, we had lots of grains and hay, of course, most of that is, you know, for the hay crop and all. Oh yeah, we raised anything, anything we could put in the ground. We used to raise barleys, rye, oats, wheat. Wheat. Wheat. Yeah. That was it. That was all, you know. I always had to kind of watch the, had to kind of watch the water as it went down, if it planted anything below the track where the dam had took in, where it had to wait for the water to see it down enough that you could get in there and then go ahead and plant into old. I remember doing that myself, but then on the upper side you used to plant that mostly in fall wheat. And then she raised one heck of a garden. Well, oh yeah. She raised a garden there. Year-round food, you know. It wouldn't even compare to what the people raised now. I always say, you know, outside of the sugar, the flour, the soap, and, you know, stuff that, stuff that we had to buy. Otherwise everything else, everything, you know, vegetables, the wall, fruit. The fruit, I tell you, now, my in-laws, They had a lot of feed there, no kidding. And we kept them going, too, you know, and took care of them. His dad used to trim them, you know, and spray them whenever, you know. Oh yeah, we, Dad died the year of the 26th. You know. Did they use horse-drawn equipment then? The horses. Everything. Everything. The horses working plowing, herring, wagon going, war machine. Oh, yeah. Oh, I worked horses on there after that and then after... Come here, honey. I worked horses there for... Working plow and even combine with the horses. Still the old binder's still sitting up there, you know, where the fellow... He moved it though, he took it out. Yeah, that was something when his dad bought it, you know, something to... Well, there was a lot of money in them days, all the farming and gearing, you know. But that's all the work I ever done on that farm. I never had no tractor, no nothing. All I done was with a plow, with a hand plow, and a set of lines across your neck and plowed through it with a single plow. Got the Elmer done, you know, when Elmer... Well, when Elmer then he finally got... My big brother, you know. I don't know if you know or not, Butch Kaiser. Oh yeah, I know her. Yeah, you know him. Well, when Butch, then finally we bought one of them, one of the first tractors that we come out with, John Deere, and that was nothing that you could pull a two-bottom plow, you know, or something like that, you see, and it wasn't too bad, but all the work that I ever done, boy, I'll tell you, everything, grubbing and pulling the stumps, and I slashed all around the aulers and everything right below where Phillip lives. You remember you and I walked down there that day and I told you? All around the whole place. Garrett, come here, honey. I cut it down first and burned it and then I pulled the stumps out with the horses. Lots of times I had to dig. Well, yeah, that was a stump puller. You know what a stump puller is? Mm-hmm. Well, I drove that, or the horses, you know, had to have horses, you know, get around with it when his dad first started clearing. No kidding. How much land did you clear? Well, all that's cleared, if you notice up there, you know, all that, that all had to be, that was all wilderness when my in-laws moved out. There wasn't too much cleared. Well, Bush got that all the way on the back of Joe's there, and then I got that all along the side of it, and I cleaned the, right there was a pump house there, I cleaned all that. You can see the picture, The old picture of the place. But a lot of that, that dad, I mean, he was young, otherwise he'd have probably had it done, you know. And that would have had to been all done by hand, too. Well, that's right. And the horses. And the members. We cut, Johnny. That cordwood cut, you had to clear. Remember, you could. We had to, what was the name, get deadlocked and then preach the sun. You know, for a room and board lots of times, you know, or maybe very little, you know, for wages. And then cordwood, that's a long, what do you call it? Four-foot. Used to get only two and a half a cord and had to be delivered, besides your dad used to deliver, used to load it on on the freight car here, you know, they take it to Hildard, I don't know if you remember that or not, you know, haul it, you know, on the fletcars, what do you call them? Lutford. Yeah, load them on there, cordwood. Yeah, for two and a half. That's what his dad was getting. And of course, washing it and towel it and fix it and haul it even. Yeah, and that's why I say, you know, wasn't – but then that was it. That was for everybody that, you know, same thing, going at it. Atencio You were here before the train came, weren't you, before the railroad? Martinez-I don't know the railroad was here. Oh, yes. I came here in 1903. Friend-The railroad was here then? Martinez-Yes. That went through our place. And the railroad is still there, because no prints now outside of the local – what is it? FRIEDMAN Yeah, local. LITTLE Yes, they are. Well, that railroad was built according to his grandmother the year of the 94. The railroad went through there. FRIEDMAN 1894. LITTLE Yeah, 1894, because I remember her saying the high water hit here, you know, covered everything, whatever, you know, all. And they used to – they had a rowboat, and go even to here, little, meet the little, little, Preacher River, some days, you know, there was any, where you'd, you'd go by rowboat, and anchor the boat right there where the house is now. You know. That was before the railroad track was there. Before the railroad. And then they had to raise it. They had, and then that's it. Well, no, the railroad was built that same year, I think, when the high water hit. Over flooded. Over flooded, yeah. And then they had to raise it up some more, like it is now. The original house was almost a half a mile from the center of the river. Yeah. Well, see, even in 1948, the year of the high water. It didn't flood the railroad track, but where the water backed in from the culverts and everything, it flooded the highway. It was above the highway. That was all covered. The highway in 1948. That was... That was high water, yeah. That hasn't been too many years. No, the highway was built the year of 1929. When they put that road through. But then they had to raise it on account of, you know... Sure, they had to raise it. That went through our place. The road went through our place also, the railroad, you know, and the power line, the Bonneville, everything. Our place seemed like it was a target for all. Well, it was, it was right next to the main, main... Yeah. Jerry, come here, honey. I said, Jerry, when you put up your garden and things, and meat and stuff, as you can, how'd you prepare the food? You can't really, everything, you know. You know, the vegetables or fruit or, oh yeah, jelly making. We had fruit trees there, crappie trees, beautiful, you know, and they were in bloom, oh yeah. Everything, you can't. Oh yeah. And I remember now the older pioneers of the Bolster Souls today, oh look at Johnny, don't bother, he likes to see something, poor little thing, you know he gets a little bit wound up, then again, You know what I heard on TV today, I take in a lot of what comes on. This doctor, you know this doctor I was telling now to Mike, I say, against licking children. Now I take it all in what comes on on the darn TV, but no kidding. But he did this talk, he did say on Good Morning America, the program, you know, from 7 to 9. I watched an hour of it, helping on in and out. But he did say, he said, you'll believe, on Licking children. Did you used to lick John? I don't think they got too doggone many lickings. You know, I never licked any of my grandsons or grandchildren, and I never licked Phillip or Gloria, which I probably should have a little bit more. Oh, shoot. But I don't ever remember getting a licking from Mom. I really, you know what I used to do? When I thought, well, maybe I'm not, I'm not going to lick him, but I, I remember that. I used to tell their dad, I said, why don't you give him a good Lincoln? He says, why me? What's the matter with you? How about, you started to say about the pioneers when they were, put up food and stuff. I'll tell you the truth about it, because they didn't have too much, they didn't have too much cleared, that grandma and grandpa then, you know, to do any gardening, you know, that I can remember of, not, you know, but I'll tell you what, what, where I was raised in the Anselmo family, before I got married to his dad, I'll tell you, there was no jars to can anything, and I can still remember it. They used, you know, like vegetable, like beans, and they, I can still remember that. They used to have them crocked, you know, what all sizes, different sizes. They blanched the veg, like beans, you know, the vegetables, you know, mostly beans, you know, like cabbage, you know, historical. They used to pit them. Well, we used to pit the cabbage, I do remember. I remember that when I was seven years old. Yeah, and that was years earlier that I can remember yet. But blanch those beans and then take them, you know, out of the boiling water and I'll put them on something, I was quite young then. And then by gum, you know, they used to put them down in crocks, you know, a layer of them blanched beans, sprinkling of salt and a little garlic, you know, cut into it, you know, that would give them a good flavor, you know, with garlic, you know. Okay. And keep it up, you know, until you, whatever amount you had. And then, and then put a press, you know, leave a plate, you know, that would fit whatever size jar, crock it was, a little rock, you know, on top of it, of course a cloth mixture. Preserved them, you know, that vegetables like, you know, like beans, the cabbage was, you know, put, you know, take care of them that way. Beets, I don't remember any, I don't remember beets raising so much. Well, I think you used to can them, Bob, I remember. No, no canning, not my days. But they used to bury them in the ground. Oh, yeah, that's right. I used to pick them with a cabbage and a carrot. You had the beets also, you know, everything, you know. And then, of course, now in the winter months, if you wanted to, you had to go and dig up either the clear the snow away and make a little, or wait until spring comes, you know, for that. That stuff. I don't care. Peppers? You remember the root house, you remember the back of the grain reach, and you used to have a walk-in, everything was covered up, and you could put stuff in there and I'll tell you it'd keep just as good if you had it in a can or anything. It'd be nice and solid. You mean ours? Yeah. We didn't have a root house, we had the cellar down in the high downstairs. Yeah, but now there was a root house behind the greenery, you remember? Right behind the greenery, you know. Oh, yeah, but I don't remember. Remember that nice root house there you used to have? And then you, I can remember that myself. Dad used to cover it up with straw and it was all buried, see? It was kind of covered up. I betcha I know what you mean. Your dad used to pit potatoes in the well. Anything. I remember how that was done. Work out the dirt and then leave about, well, the bottom of it, not this high, you know, a ridge around. 6 inches. And then, you know, put your potatoes in there, you know, if I'm in a manta, I'll walk them in the sacks, of course, and then cover them up with straw, and then pile up the dirt. Good. No kidding. What the, you know. I still do that right out here. What is he doing? What do you, what do you... That's the early days. And pickles now, the pickling, the pickling making, them days they didn't, the older folks, bless their souls, I say, there was no deal, because there was no such a thing as they knew what deal, you know. The Germans, that's on my husband's side, you know. The deal, that was it. To this day, you pickle, make any deal pickles, you know, you gotta have that deal, you know. We used to raise it, pull it out like weeds. No kidding. But they used to pickle them, make a brine vinegar, water, and then the salt, you know, whatever. They knew how. And put them in a crock or else in a barrel, you know, oak, you know, them days, you know. If it was a ten-gallon or fifteen, it all, you know, depend how much, yeah. And they keep, they keep through until they're lasted. Yeah, no kidding. Peppers, do you ever eat any pickled peppers? Okay, they used to put them in that, with the cucumbers, you know, and the peppers, and yeah, and stuff like that, all in one barrel. Cauliflower, put them all in there in that brine. You had to cover it up, you know, so no dust and dirt to her mouth didn't twist. Okay, that's it. But okay. Did you see how hard to keep the house clean at the time because of the mud and... Well, pretty good, you know, just work from morning until hard-telling, you know, in the evening, milk and cow soup. There's a kind of jar she canned with. Yeah, where'd you get that? With the glass top. With the glass top, no, no, I found these in, but yeah, she used to have the half gallons and, and, I was just a kid, I was shooting deer and you talk about taking care of your meat where you never had no refrigeration. You used to can as high as either maybe 50, 60 half gallons and maybe another hundred. Make meatballs too, you know. Make the meat balls. Especially when we had the pork at the same time. See the deer, we used to butcher what time? Fall of the year? November sometime? And then the deer, you know, the meat, you know, and then grind it up, you know, mix pork in with it too, you know, and then season it up and bite. How'd you do that? Did you cook the meat first? No, we didn't. Just put it, you know, you make them, you know, the regular, you know, and put them in, fill up your jars, you know, with a little garlic. A garlic I'll be all this day, even John, you ate it. And by gum, you know, they make their own juice, you know. Had to boil it, though, you know, you know, about three to four hours, you know. I used to have a boiler, you know, to do all my canning, you know. Or at one time, at the early days of the other poor souls, put the fruit cooker in an open kettle, have your jars hot, okay, your lids hot, you know what I mean, you know, located, that was it. Done okay, done okay, you know. But then of course the later years and the later years, you know, then, you know, got down to, you know, to steam them, you know what I say, you know, but that's not a thing. Oh yeah, I had 16, 16 quarts for, you know, batch cooking each time. Had a copper boiler, you remember? Oh yeah. Copper, copper boiler, and had those put away, you know, for that, for the canning, you know. Oh yeah, used to make, I used to make our own sausage, pork sausage, and everything was all made. We used to render out our own lard. Yeah, everything had to be, you know, to do all of that on our own. Otherwise, you know, oh yeah, well that's okay. That was all done and it was good. So that's it. That's why I say now, Johnny goes down naturally. But we had, we had my mother-in-law at home, you know, that she took care of, and the kids were small, but I used to go out in the field and help his dad, even, even as cheap as the wages were too, you know, but then it wasn't so easy to get the money either. But, just go down and help. Yeah, cutting, cutting with a mower machine, you know, drive the horses, the mower or the rake, the wagon. Yeah. Oh, sure. We used to help, you know, we used to stack the grain, you know, when it was ready cut, you know, and then you had to stack it until you used to get the, Was it Uncle Henry then that had that machine? Or was somebody else with a machine? The old time conveyor deal, you know, you had to... You built your... When you cut your wheat and everything, the old time binder, and you had bundles about like that, and you used to make a kind of a pyramid that'd come up like that. So, it'd keep the rain, and then when the time comes to go ahead and thrash, well, then you'd have to stay out there and feed it and adjust it. You'd pull your trash machine to the stack, you know, somebody in the... Or run with an old steam engine, you know. And I remember another little, another way of thrashing, and my early, that I used to have, I used to get the bundles, you know, I used to make a big outdoor, you know, kind of a, oh, what do you call it in the house? I'd, and had to thrash it with the horses. And I don't mean maybe you could throw your bundles, how many at a time, in that inside to something around. And by doggone, you know, to stamp them, you know, them bundles, you know, with the horses. But that was... Now that there was something. That was a job there. How to clean that grain, you know, to get it, you know. Of course it was for the chickens and the house, or the whatever we fed it to, you know. But that's the way, that was the threshing down my young days that I remember that. Because I remember, I remember driving, you know, the horses, you know, to, you know, round and round and round, you know, so they stamped that to green. Huh, so I say, push buttons today, why not? Gosh, goodness, everything good. Don't have to go like that. Did you have ice house? Yes, we did. Yeah. I can remember going down and cutting ice myself down there. And we used to drive the old sleigh out there with a team of horses on the ice. It used to be 18, 20 inches thick and go out there with a handsaw. You'd be a good boy. And the old crosscut, not a handsaw, the old crosscut saw and cut them into blocks and then I've still got a pair of the ice tongs and pick them out and put them up and load them in a sleigh and then have the old log cabin ice house filled with sawdust and you'd have it all out of there and you put your layer down and you'd have to, every space that was where you laid your block ice down, well then you chopped it all up with the sawdust and then you put a layer of sawdust on top of that and put your next layer of ice on it and keep chopping it up until you have it filled up and you'd have ice till all summer long. All summer. The sawdust, I don't know what meal was it that your dad used to get the sawdust and I, but then the, whatever, we had it. But there was winters there wasn't any ice to cut, there wasn't any. Mild winters and never used to freeze up for any ice cutting. Yeah. Well, yeah, So we had to do without it. I remember coming, when I first, when I first come in here, I had my grandmother's. The ice box. My mother-in-law. The ice box, I had it sitting right in the corner there, and I had a coal drill down through the floor where the pipe come down, the overflow and go down. And I used to go up and buy ice from old Hanson up there. Yeah. And then they, I mean, after that the ice up there at the ranch, it went to pot and everything. But that's what I first started out with right here. And he had it up there all the time. Well, we had an icebox here Uncle Henry built himself, because he was a pretty good carpenter, his uncle, Henry Kaiser. And he had built one, you know, and then had a galvanized tank, you know, in the middle of the big, you know, like a cupboard, And then a pipe, you know, through the water would run down. You had to have a pan underneath to catch the water, or, you know, had to watch it. He asked for like, him to... Well, you know, he's a good boy. Yeah, Mike's gonna tell your daddy you were a good boy. Is that alright? Cause your daddy told me that Mike was gonna come over. How's that? I said, I don't know if I can help Mike out anymore because I said I don't know him that much anymore. How's that? Maybe you tell him something. You tell Mike what you know. What do you know? Tell him. See birds in the trees? That's good. Sure. Your senator was in Grandma's yard this morning, your Uncle Bob's. You know Robert Keyser? No, I don't. Well, yeah. Well, I don't know. You know, I mean, my question is... My senator in your yard? Well, oh, you're... I give him nicknames. Oh, no, poor Bill. Well, you worked down here at the... No. Well, you didn't work down here at the Times? BF Well yeah, I was the editor of the Times. LW That's where I was working. LW Well who, because I don't know now, who is running at the… BF John White I think has it now. LW They bought out? BF Well Pete Thompson bought it out. LW Oh I see. BF John White's the editor now. LW Well you know, we subscribe, excuse me. His dad and I were the first ones, the first subscription on the Priest River Times, 1914. That was on our wedding day, the 15th day of April, 1914. And that's the first they give us, that's our wedding gift. Mr. Fuzzy was his name, went around picking up Subscriptions and so on subscribers, whatever you say. Yeah at our wedding day. No kidding. We had that paper ever since, never missed one. How many years, how long was this? Figure it out for yourself. 1914 and this is, what? 1979. No kidding. Well, you know, there's been quite several of them, editors here, you know. Here, you know. Schimhorn, he was one. Do you remember? He said, have you got it on your... Schimhorn? Yeah, that's the only work I remember. Parsons and Harrison and Wickwares, you know, that I can, I, you know, remember? And let's see who else have their signatures here. Well, Woods had it. Well, but who was... Yeah, Woods. I suppose a lot of parts of the year, but Wickware and... Yeah. Three or four of them that I can remember. Well, yeah, Tim Horn. He was an old, He was the first, I think he was the first one when I first issued the paper. Oh yeah. That was 1914. And we had it ever since, every dollar a copy of them days. A dollar a what? A dollar a year, probably. A dollar a year, yeah. Yeah, that's what I mean. Yeah, isn't that something? Well, alright. I haven't raised too much though, I mean in my life. What is it, seven dollars? I got in on this special, that was for something I think for one year. So I just went down and Robert went down and paid it quite as well. They would have given me the free, if I had put them up on it. Marie, Woods, Connie, the last guy we were working with. Oh yeah, because they were there too, you know. Not Marie, but Connie. Connie and who else it working there? Ed Hensher's wife.

lines.line: # Introduction and Historical Context
> Introduction of the interview setting, including the date and location of the interview in Priester, Idaho. The interviewer is Michael R. Anderson.
** interview, history, Louisa Keyser, Michael R. Anderson, Priester, Idaho, 1979 **

lines.line: # Early Days and Family History
> Discussion of the early days, relying on candle lights, the history of her in-laws, and family life dating back to the 1880s. The importance of making do with limited resources is emphasized.
** candle lights, family history, 1880s, survival, in-laws **
keywords: ** candle lights, family history, 1880s, survival, in-laws **

lines.line: # Cooking and Bread Making
> Louisa describes the challenges of cooking on a wood stove, the necessity of gathering wood for the winter, and baking bread from scratch.
** wood stove, cooking, bread baking, winter wood, survival **
keywords: ** wood stove, cooking, bread baking, winter wood, survival **

metadata.title: Cooking and Bread Making

lines.line: # Self-Sufficiency and Food Preparation
> Insights into the importance of buying flour in bulk, storing it over winter, and the reliance on horses and sleds for transportation.
** self-sufficiency, flour, food storage, horses, transportation **

lines.line: # Raising Crops and Food Preservation
> Details of the various crops raised, including grains and hay. The chapter also explores the garden's role in providing year-round food.
** crops, grains, hay, food preservation, garden **

children.text: # Introduction and Historical Context
> Introduction of the interview setting, including the date and location of the interview in Priester, Idaho. The interviewer is Michael R. Anderson.
** interview, history, Louisa Keyser, Michael R. Anderson, Priester, Idaho, 1979 **
# Early Days and Family History
> Discussion of the early days, relying on candle lights, the history of her in-laws, and family life dating back to the 1880s. The importance of making do with limited resources is emphasized.
** candle lights, family history, 1880s, survival, in-laws **
# Cooking and Bread Making
> Louisa describes the challenges of cooking on a wood stove, the necessity of gathering wood for the winter, and baking bread from scratch.
** wood stove, cooking, bread baking, winter wood, survival **
# Self-Sufficiency and Food Preparation
> Insights into the importance of buying flour in bulk, storing it over winter, and the reliance on horses and sleds for transportation.
** self-sufficiency, flour, food storage, horses, transportation **
# Land Ownership and Family Roots
> A detailed account of land ownership, the selling of lots by Louisa's grandparents, and the family's move to Spokane in the 1880s.
** land ownership, Spokane, family roots, property sale **
# Agricultural Work and Harvest
> Louisa discusses the number of acres owned, the crops grown on their land, and the extensive labor involved in farming and land maintenance.
** agriculture, harvest, farm work, land ownership, crops **
